summary:
Alexander the Great's Empire (336–323 BC) was the vast military and political dominion created through thirteen years of virtually unbroken campaigning by Alexander III of Macedon — history's most celebrated conqueror — stretching from Greece and Egypt in the west through Persia and Central Asia to the Indus Valley in the east. At its greatest extent, it encompassed roughly two million square miles and contained perhaps a third of the world's population, making it the largest empire the world had yet seen.

Alexander inherited from his father [[event:1028|Philip II of Macedon]] a superbly trained army, a secured Greece bound by the [[event:1030|League of Corinth]], and an ambitious plan to avenge the Persian Wars by attacking the Achaemenid Empire. He crossed into Asia in 334 BC with roughly 40,000 troops and never returned to Macedonia alive. His campaign unfolded through a series of decisive victories — [[event:1031|Battle of Granicus]] against a Persian provincial force, [[event:1032|Battle of Issus]] against Darius III himself, [[event:1033|Siege of Tyre]] establishing naval supremacy, [[event:1035|Battle of Gaugamela]] destroying the Persian imperial army — that dismantled the Achaemenid Empire in just five years.

Alexander's genius combined personal courage with sophisticated logistics, flexible tactical thinking, and an ability to inspire extraordinary loyalty in his troops. He personally led cavalry charges that were calculated to produce decisive psychological shock — he was wounded repeatedly in combat. But he was more than a battlefield commander: he recognized the political necessity of presenting himself to Persians and Egyptians in culturally appropriate ways, adopting Persian court dress and Egyptian divine titles, and seeking to integrate conquered elites rather than simply replacing them.

The [[event:1034|Foundation of Alexandria]] in Egypt became the most enduring monument of the campaign — one of more than twenty cities Alexander founded that served as administrative centers and nodes of Greek cultural diffusion. The [[event:80|Hellenistic Period]] of Greek cultural dominance across the Near East was directly created by his campaigns, as thousands of Greek settlers followed in the army's wake and Greek became the common language of educated discourse from Egypt to Bactria.

Alexander's ambitions showed no sign of satisfaction even as the empire expanded. The [[event:1036|Alexander in India]] campaign pushed into the Punjab despite the exhaustion of his troops, ending only when his men refused to march further after the [[event:1037|Battle of Hydaspes]]. He turned back through the Gedrosian desert in a march that killed thousands, then spent his last years planning further expeditions to Arabia and possibly the western Mediterranean.

His [[event:1038|Death of Alexander the Great]] at Babylon in June 323 BC, at age thirty-two, from fever (possibly combined with heavy drinking and the cumulative effects of wounds), abruptly ended the empire's expansion. Without a clear heir and successor, the empire immediately fractured among his generals in the [[event:1039|Wars of the Diadochi]]. No single successor was able to reunify it. The lasting consequences were cultural rather than political: the spread of the Greek language and Hellenistic civilization created the cultural medium through which, centuries later, Christianity would spread and in which the New Testament would be written.

Alexander's reputation in antiquity was immense and paradoxical — simultaneously the liberator who freed Greeks from Persia and the conqueror who subjugated them to Macedonian rule, the visionary of cultural fusion and the destroyer of Persepolis. His legend shaped Julius Caesar, Napoleon, and countless others who measured their achievements against his impossible standard.
